name: Honda Accord (11th Gen)
engine: 1.5L turbo I4 (L15BE), 192 hp — 2.0L Atkinson I4 + two-motor hybrid, 204 hp combined
fuel_economy: 29 city / 37 hwy mpg (1.5T) · ~46 city / 41 hwy mpg (Hybrid)
reliability: 9/10 — too new for a deep field record, but it carries Honda's proven 1.5T and the well-developed two-motor hybrid system, which is now the volume choice. Dropping the 2.0T and the manual narrows the lineup to efficient commuters. Early recalls have been minor (verify any open campaign by VIN).
cost_to_own: Insurance: Average · Maintenance ~$400/yr · The hybrid's real-world economy is the headline running-cost win; the battery carries a long warranty
popular_mods: All-weather liners and tasteful wheels with quality tires
Winter tires on a second set for cold-climate owners
Sound-deadening / interior tweaks for the long-commute crowd
Honest take: this generation is about efficiency and refinement — it's a commuter, not a mod platform

generations.11th Gen (2023+): A simpler, hybrid-led lineup: a 1.5L turbo on lower trims and a stronger 204hp two-motor Hybrid on Sport/EX-L/Touring, which is now the volume seller. A larger Google built-in infotainment system up top; the old 2.0T and manual are gone.
generations.Hybrid-first strategy: Honda positioned the hybrid as the mainstream choice here, trading the previous 2.0T's outright pace for efficiency and refinement.

common_issues: Too new for a deep reliability record — track open recalls by VIN
Some early infotainment / Google built-in software quirks
More tire and road noise than a luxury sedan — a refinement gripe
Standard early-production fit-and-finish items, addressed under warranty
maintenance_tips: Keep the infotainment software updated on early cars
Run 0W-20 full synthetic on the Maintenance Minder
The hybrid battery is covered by a long warranty — no special owner upkeep beyond regular service
Cabin filter, brakes, and tire rotations are most of the routine cost
